content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 18, 1990


Norman Sato
Deputy City Attorney, San Jose
Office of City Attorney
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-325

Dear Mr. Sato:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of San Jose City Councilmember Shirley Lewis regarding her
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Since your advice request
does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.2/ Please,
note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate
any conduct which may have already taken place.

QUESTION

What are Councilmember Lewis' disclosure and disqualification
responsibilities with respect to a partnership in which she has
invested as a limited partner, and with respect to the general
partners of that partnership?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Lewis must disqualify herself from
participating in any city council decision which will have a

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-325
Page 2

reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or both of
her general partners, either personally or through other business
entities. In addition, the councilmember must disclose her
interest in the Stockton partnership because the Stockton
partnership is doing business in her jurisdiction.

FACTS

Councilmember Lewis has an investment interest in a
partnership which is active in real estate transactions in the
City of Stockton. The partnership consists of two general
partners and 15 limited partners, of which Councilmember Lewis is
one of the limited partners. Each limited partner has invested
more than $2,000 in the partnership. You stated that
Councilmember Lewis' interest constitutes less than 10% of the
partnership.

The two general partners are also active in the San Jose
area. You stated that the general partners maintain a different
partnership in San Jose. The only connection to the Stockton
partnership is the fact that the same general partners control
both partnerships and the administrative aspects of the Stockton
partnership are handled at the offices of the San Jose
partnership.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act (the "Act"), was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act was
to ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed,
would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias
caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests
of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in mak-
ing, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence
a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A "public official" is defined in Section 82048 and
Regulation 18700 (copy enclosed) as every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. As a San Jose City Councilmember,
Councilmember Lewis is a public official as defined in the Act.

Economic Interests

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

File No. I-90-325
Page 3


     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

                    Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).

     Thus, Councilmember Lewis may not make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which she knows or has reason to know she
has an financial interest. (Section 87100.)

     In your letter you described the councilmember's sole
economic interest in question as an investment in the Stockton
partnership. You stated that Councilmember Lewis has an
investment in the Stockton partnership of more than $1,000, and is
a limited partner in the partnership.3/ However, Section 82034
defines "investment" for purposes of the Act to include only
financial interests if the business entity or any parent,
subsidiary or otherwise related business entity has an interest in
real property in the jurisdiction, or does business or plans to do
business in the jurisdiction at any time during the two years prior to the time
any statement or other action is required under this title.4/

     Regulation 18236 (copy enclosed) defines "otherwise related
business entity" as follows:


3/ Presumably she has also received income from the partnership.
If the income was more than $250 in the last 12 months, then the
income is an additional grounds for considering the partnership
itself as an economic interest of the councilmember.

4/ You stated in your facts that Councilmember Lewis does not own
10% or more of the partnership. Please note that if the
councilmember's interest does become 10% or more, a pro-rata share
of investments of the partnership will be treated as investment
interests of the councilmember. (Section 82034.)

File No. I-90-325
Page 4


     (b) Business entities, including corpora-
tions, partnerships, joint ventures and any other
organizations and enterprises operated for profit,
which do not have a parent-subsidiary relationship
are otherwise related if any one of the following
three tests is met:

          (1) One business entity has a control-
ling ownership interest in the other business
entity.

          (2) There is shared management and
control between the entities. In determining
whether there is shared management and
control, consideration should be given to the
following factors:

               (A) The same person or
substantially the same person owns and
manages the two entities;

               (B) There are common or commingled
funds or assets;

               (C) The business entities share the
use of the same offices or employees, or
otherwise share activities, resources or
personnel on a regular basis;

               (D) There is otherwise a regular
and close working relationship between
the entities; or

          (3) A controlling owner (50% or greater
interest as a shareholder or as a general
partner)5/ in one entity also is a controlling
owner in the other entity.

               Regulation 18236, footnote and
emphasis added.

     Thus, if either the Stockton partnership or the San Jose
partnership which is otherwise related to the Stockton partnership
does business in San Jose, the councilmember's interest in the
Stockton partnership would be disclosable. You stated that the
administrative activities of the Stockton partnership are handled
at the San Jose Offices of the San Jose partnership. We believe
that this link alone would be sufficient to meet the "doing
business" requirement of the statute. While such activity might

5/ In 1985, the Commission adopted a policy interpreting a
controlling ownership interest as an ownership interest of more
than 50%.

File No. I-90-325
Page 5


not constitute "doing business" in the jurisdiction under other
provisions of law, we believe that these other actions are not
determinative because the other standards were enacted for
purposes different than the purposes of personal financial
disclosure under the Act. (In re Baty (1979) 5 FPPC Ops. 10, copy
enclosed.)

     An additional reason for concluding that the Stockton
partnership is an investment interest of the councilmember is
because the San Jose partnership is otherwise related to the
Stockton partnership due to the same general partners. According
to your facts the San Jose partnership has interests in real
property and does business in the councilmember's jurisdiction.
Consequently, the Stockton partnership is an investment interest
of the councilmember. (Section 82034.)

     Further, Regulation 18706 (copy enclosed) provides that for
conflict of interest purposes an official has a financial interest
in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on a business entity which is otherwise related to a
business entity in which the official has a financial interest.
(Regulation 18236.) Thus, Councilmember Lewis has an economic
interest in the partnership in San Jose because that partnership
has the same general partners.

     However, the analysis does not end with the respective
business entities. In 1983 in In re Nord (8 FPPC Ops. 6, copy
enclosed) the Commission addressed a similar question concerning
the Merced City Attorney and his interest in a limited
partnership. In that opinion the Commission considered the roles
of the partners in a limited partnership. The Commission stated:
"The general partners have full control over the management of the
firm [and are] personally responsible for the debts and
obligations of the firm, as in the case of ordinary partnerships,
without regard to the amounts contributed by his or her to the
firm capital...The liability of the general partner for the debts
of the firm has been held...to extend not only to his or her
interest in the partnership but to all his or her private
property." (Nord, supra, p. 8.)

     Conversely, "the limited partners are not bound by the
obligations of the partnership, thus their liability is
'limited.'" (Nord, supra, p. 8.) Thus, the limited partnership
framework "involves an investment by the limited partners in the
entrepreneurial skills of the general partner, who is charged with
the sole discretion and authority to manage the investment by
conducting the partnership business." (Nord, supra, p. 9.) "In
doing so the limited partner is really investing in the general
partner" personally. (Nord, supra, p. 12.)

     The Commission concluded in light of the Act's clear
intention to prohibit conflicts of interest arising from financial

File No. I-90-325
Page 6


relationships, that an investment by a limited partner in the
partnership also is an investment in the controlling general
partners. Thus Councilmember Lewis also has an economic interest
in the general partners of the partnership individually.

In summation, Councilmember Lewis has an economic interest in
the Stockton partnership, the general partners of the Stockton
partnership personally and in any business entity that is
otherwise related to the Stockton partnership, including the San
Jose partnership.

Disqualification

For an economic interest to be disqualifying with respect to
a specific governmental decision, it must be reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the economic interest. Consequently, Councilmember Lewis may
not participate in any decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect on any
economic interest.

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed.)

In addition, the foreseeable financial effect on the
councilmember's economic interest must also be material to require
disqualification. The test for materiality differs depending on
the specific circumstances of each decision. For example, where
an specific interest is directly before the city council, the
effect is generally deemed to be material and disqualification is
required.6/ (Regulation 18702.1(a), copy enclosed.)

Where an economic interest is not directly before the city
council, but may be indirectly affected, Regulations 18702.2
through 18702.6 apply. Regulations 18702.2 and 18702.6 (copies
enclosed) deal specifically with sources of income and

_________________________

6/ An economic interest is directly before the city council when
the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named
party in, or the subject of, the proceeding. A person or business
entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person or business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

File No.  I-90-325
Page 7


investments.  We have enclosed a conflict-of-interest pamphlet
which discusses these standards, for your information.

Disclosure

     Under the Act, every public official must disclose all his or
her investments, real property interests, sources of income and
sources of gifts that could foreseeably be affected by the
exercise of the official's duties.  (Codes 81002(c), 87200-
87313.)   Consequently, the Stockton partnership should be
disclosed on Councilmember Lewis' statement of economic interest
because the partnership is a business entity in which the
councilmember has an investment interest of greater than $1,000
and is doing business in her jurisdiction.  The councilmember need
not disclose the separate San Jose partnership nor the individual
partners despite the fact that they are potentially disqualifying
economic interests.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:dg

Enclosures

The two general partners are also active in the San Jose area. You stated that the general partners maintain a different partnership in San Jose. The only connection to the Stockton partnership is the fact that the same general partners control both partnerships and the administrative aspects of the Stockton partnership are handled at the offices of the San Jose partnership. ANALYSIS The Political Reform Act (the "Act"), was enacted by the people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them.
